icd_category: M08.30 – Juvenile rheumatoid polyarthritis (seronegative), unspecified site
one_liner: A 6-year-old female with no significant past medical history presents with 8 weeks of progressive bilateral knee and wrist swelling, morning stiffness lasting over 1 hour, and a daily spiking fever with a salmon-colored evanescent rash.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient's mother reports the child first complained of bilateral knee pain 8 weeks ago. Over the following weeks, swelling appeared in both knees, both wrists, and several small joints of the hands. She has marked morning stiffness lasting 1.5-2 hours that gradually improves with activity throughout the day. Her gait has become stiff and she avoids running. Over the past 3 weeks, she has developed daily fevers spiking to 39-40°C, typically in the late afternoon/evening, with spontaneous return to normal or below normal by morning (quotidian fever pattern). A faint pink rash appears on her trunk and proximal extremities during fever spikes and fades when the fever breaks. She has had a 1.5 kg weight loss and generalized fatigue. There is no sore throat, diarrhea, or recent travel. No tick exposure. No preceding trauma.
stage2.pmh: No significant past medical history. Born full-term. Up to date on immunizations.
stage2.medications: Ibuprofen 200 mg PO TID (10 mg/kg/dose) with minimal improvement
stage2.family_history: Maternal aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. No family history of psoriasis, inflammatory bowel disease, or other autoimmune conditions.
stage2.social_history: Lives with both parents. Attends 1st grade but has missed 2 weeks due to symptoms. Previously active in ballet but stopped due to joint pain. No pets. No travel history.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Alert, appears fatigued, mildly pale; weight loss evident. Neck: Supple, no cervical lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Mildly tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; no pericardial friction rub. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Mild hepatosplenomegaly (liver 3 cm below costal margin, spleen tip palpable); No tenderness. Musculoskeletal: Bilateral knee effusions with warmth and limited range of motion (flexion limited to 100°); bilateral wrist swelling with tenderness and decreased dorsiflexion; MCP joints 2-4 bilaterally swollen. Skin: Faint salmon-pink, macular, evanescent rash on trunk and proximal extremities, with central clearing — the rash blanches with pressure and appeared coincident with fever spike; the rash is NOT pruritic; Lymph nodes: Generalized lymphadenopathy (bilateral axillary and inguinal, 1-2 cm, non-tender, mobile).

lab_results.clinical_significance: Markedly elevated ESR reflects significant systemic inflammation characteristic of systemic JIA.
Markedly elevated CRP confirms active systemic inflammation.
Markedly elevated ferritin is characteristic of systemic JIA and is a key marker to monitor for macrophage activation syndrome (MAS) — ferritin >10,000 ng/mL raises high suspicion for MAS.
Leukocytosis with neutrophilia, anemia of chronic disease, and reactive thrombocytosis are typical of systemic JIA.

lab_results.result: Normochromic normocytic anemia. Reactive neutrophilia. Reactive thrombocytosis. No blasts. No atypical lymphocytes.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Absence of blasts is critical to exclude leukemia, which is the most important malignant mimic of systemic JIA.
RF is negative in most JIA subtypes; when positive in this condition JIA, it is associated with a more aggressive, erosive course similar to adult RA.
ANA is typically negative in systemic JIA; a positive ANA in oligoarticular JIA increases risk of chronic anterior uveitis.
Negative anti-CCP, consistent with this condition disease.
Mildly elevated LDH reflects tissue inflammation and cell turnover.
Elevated D-dimer in systemic JIA warrants monitoring for MAS; markedly elevated D-dimer with falling platelets and fibrinogen would suggest evolving MAS.
Negative blood cultures help exclude bacterial sepsis and endocarditis as causes of fever with arthritis.

imaging_results.result: Bilateral knee and wrist joint effusions. Periarticular soft tissue swelling. Periarticular osteopenia. No joint space narrowing. No erosions. Growth plates appear normal.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Soft tissue swelling and periarticular osteopenia without erosions are typical early findings of JIA; erosions develop later with uncontrolled disease.

imaging_results.result: Normal biventricular function. Small pericardial effusion (4 mm). No valvular abnormalities. No vegetations.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Small pericardial effusion (serositis) is common in systemic JIA and helps differentiate from other JIA subtypes; no evidence of endocarditis.

imaging_results.result: Mild cardiomegaly (likely due to pericardial effusion). No pleural effusions. Clear lung fields.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Mild cardiomegaly from pericardial effusion; serositis is a feature of systemic JIA.

low_yield_tests: MRI of joints (not first-line for diagnosis)

stage5.final_clue: Markedly elevated inflammatory markers with a high ferritin and negative RF/ANA complete the picture. Bone marrow biopsy (if pursued) shows reactive changes without blasts.

debrief.classic_presentation: Systemic JIA (formerly Still's disease) presents in children <16 years with arthritis and prominent systemic features: quotidian (daily spiking) fever that reaches ≥39°C once or twice daily and returns to baseline or below, characteristic evanescent salmon-pink macular rash that appears with fever spikes, hepatosplenomegaly, generalized lymphadenopathy, and serositis (pericarditis/pleuritis). Lab findings show markedly elevated inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP, ferritin), leukocytosis, anemia, and thrombocytosis. RF and ANA are characteristically negative. Peak age of onset is 1-5 years with equal sex distribution.
debrief.attending_mode_notes: The most life-threatening complication is macrophage activation syndrome (MAS), a form of secondary hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) that occurs in 10-15% of systemic JIA patients. Key MAS warning signs: paradoxically FALLING ESR (despite worsening clinical status — because fibrinogen drops), markedly rising ferritin (>10,000), FALLING platelet count and WBC (previously elevated), rising LDH and liver enzymes, rising triglycerides, falling fibrinogen, and coagulopathy. MAS is a medical emergency requiring high-dose IV methylprednisolone and cyclosporine. Also critical: the differential diagnosis must always include leukemia (ALL can mimic JIA with joint pain, fever, lymphadenopathy, and hepatosplenomegaly), particularly before starting steroids — a peripheral smear and potentially bone marrow biopsy should be obtained.
debrief.pathophysiology: Systemic JIA is now classified as an autoinflammatory (rather than autoimmune) disease, driven primarily by innate immune system dysregulation. Key cytokines include IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-18, which drive the quotidian fever, systemic inflammation, and elevated acute-phase reactants. Unlike other JIA subtypes, adaptive immunity (T/B cells) plays a secondary role, explaining the absence of autoantibodies (RF, ANA negative). The pathogenesis involves macrophage and neutrophil activation with excessive cytokine production. IL-6 drives hepatic acute-phase protein synthesis (CRP, ferritin, fibrinogen, hepcidin), contributes to anemia of chronic disease (via hepcidin-mediated iron sequestration), and promotes the systemic inflammatory response.
debrief.treatment_overview: Treatment has been revolutionized by biologic therapies targeting IL-1 and IL-6: FIRST-LINE for systemic features: NSAIDs for mild disease; systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 1-2 mg/kg/day) for moderate-severe disease. BIOLOGIC AGENTS: IL-1 inhibitors (anakinra — daily SC injection; canakinumab — monthly SC injection) or IL-6 inhibitor (tocilizumab — IV or SC) are now considered early in disease course and can produce dramatic responses. Traditional DMARDs (methotrexate) are less effective for systemic features but may help with articular disease. For MAS: high-dose pulse IV methylprednisolone (30 mg/kg) + cyclosporine A; refractory MAS may require etoposide or anakinra. Long-term monitoring: ophthalmologic screening for uveitis (less common in systemic JIA than oligoarticular), growth monitoring, bone density assessment.
high_yield_pearls: The quotidian fever pattern of systemic JIA is DIAGNOSTIC: the temperature spikes to ≥39°C once or twice daily (often late afternoon/evening) and returns to NORMAL or BELOW normal between spikes — this distinguishes it from the continuous fever of infection or malignancy.

The EVANESCENT salmon-pink rash of systemic JIA is pathognomonic: it appears during fever spikes and FADES when the fever resolves; it is macular, non-pruritic, found on the trunk and proximal extremities, and can be provoked by scratching (Koebner phenomenon).

Macrophage activation syndrome (MAS) is the most dangerous complication — a FALLING ESR (dropping fibrinogen) in a worsening patient is a RED FLAG; paradoxically falling platelets and WBC (previously elevated) with skyrocketing ferritin should trigger immediate treatment.

ALWAYS exclude leukemia before starting corticosteroids in a child with fever, arthralgia, lymphadenopathy, and hepatosplenomegaly — ALL can mimic systemic JIA; obtain a peripheral smear and consider bone marrow biopsy.

IL-1 (anakinra, canakinumab) and IL-6 (tocilizumab) inhibitors have transformed systemic JIA treatment with response rates >80%, often allowing steroid sparing and preventing the devastating growth and bone effects of long-term corticosteroid use.
sources: ILAR: International League of Associations for Rheumatology Classification of JIA — https://www.uptodate.com/contents/search?search=ILAR%3A+International+League+of+Associations+for+Rheumatology+Classification+of+JIA
ACR/AF 2019: Guidelines for the Treatment of Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=ACR%2FAF+2019%3A+Guidelines+for+the+Treatment+of+Juvenile+Idiopathic+Arthritis

UpToDate: Systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis: Clinical manifestations and diagnosis — https://www.uptodate.com/contents/search?search=Systemic+juvenile+idiopathic+arthritis%3A+Clinical+manifestations+and+diagnosis
UpToDate: Systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis: Treatment — https://www.uptodate.com/contents/search?search=Systemic+juvenile+idiopathic+arthritis%3A+Treatment

after_stage2_more_likely: Systemic JIA (quotidian fever, evanescent rash, polyarthritis, hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy — classic Still's disease presentation)
ALL (overlapping features: fever, hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, joint pain, anemia)
after_stage2_less_likely: Acute rheumatic fever (no evidence of recent strep, no carditis murmur, rash pattern differs, polyarthritis of JIA is additive vs migratory in ARF)
SLE (no malar rash, no oral ulcers, no hair loss, ANA negative, age atypical for SLE)
Kawasaki disease (>5 days fever but lacks conjunctivitis, oral changes, extremity changes — and KD does not typically cause polyarthritis)
after_labs_top3: Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (must be excluded before steroids)
Macrophage activation syndrome (if ferritin rises dramatically)
